evidence_quotes.quote: disabling games that customers have already paid for is a gross abuse of the digital contract between the company and consumer.
Publishers even invoke these licenses to justify dark patterns, addictive features and unfair personalization in their products.
There appears to be monopolistic collusion in the games industry to sell titles as revocable licenses rather than owned goods, leaving consumers no choice but to accept one-sided contracts.

paragraphs.text: I would like to express my support for including a provision in the Digital Fairness Act to prevent the commercial practice of companies destroying digital products (video games). Currently, companies can unilaterally shut down games at their sole discretion without providing any recompense or recourse to the customer who paid for that product. It is understandable that an online service cannot be maintained indefinitely; however, companies should plan for this eventuality and set up ways for customers to continue using the digital product they purchased. This is not an unreasonable request, as many companies have done so of their own accord, for example, by supporting private servers. I strongly believe such a provision belongs in the Digital Fairness Act, since it directly concerns consumer protection. Specifically, disabling games that customers have already paid for is a gross abuse of the digital contract between the company and consumer. When someone purchases a game dependent on the companys continued support, they are not adequately informed that their purchase may be rendered unusable at an undisclosed time, entirely at the companys whim. This practice is widespread across the games industry, and there is no way of knowing how long the product you buy will be supported. Additionally, companies often use shutdowns to force consumers into purchasing their new products. By disabling the customer's product the company forces the customer to buy their new product to get the same experience. Game companies sell digital goods not as products, but as licenses that can be revoked at any time, for any reason (or no reason at all). This runs contrary to the idea that consumers own the products they buy and gives companies unfair control over what they have sold. Publishers even invoke these licenses to justify dark patterns, addictive features and unfair personalization in their products. I support the principle that customers own the products they buy, not the companies that sell them. The Act should include a provision preventing companies from exerting undue and unreasonable control over sold products, specifically, companies should not be allowed to shut down games without offering compensation or alternative means to play the game. A video game is a digital good composed of many components (art, textures, sound effects, music, code, etc.), and the customer pays for the use of all these components together. Yet companies claim to sell only a license entirely at their discretion. I believe this is an unfair business practice and should be restricted by the Act. Once a product is sold, the selling company should have no right to terminate it and render it unusable; the consumer should retain the right to use their product as they wish. The company retains intellectual-property rights, but the specific copy sold to the customer ought to belong to the customer, not exist at the companys discretion. This practice is highly unfair for several reasons. There appears to be monopolistic collusion in the games industry to sell titles as revocable licenses rather than owned goods, leaving consumers no choice but to accept one-sided contracts. It also enables publishers to introduce additional monetization into a product after sale, often without clear disclosure of what is and what is not included. I would like to thank the commission for taking the time to take public comments and I hope suggestions such as mine will be considered and hopefully included in the act.
